answer.text: Set User ID

feedback.text: Correct! SUID (Set User ID) is a special permission bit that allows a file to execute with the privileges of its owner rather than the user running it.

answer.text: System User ID

feedback.text: Incorrect. SUID stands for Set User ID.

answer.text: Secure Unix ID

feedback.text: Incorrect. SUID stands for Set User ID.

answer.text: Superuser Interface Daemon

feedback.text: Incorrect. SUID stands for Set User ID.

name.text: OS Security Entry Q2 — Windows Registry

answer.text: A hierarchical database that stores OS and application configuration settings

feedback.text: Correct! The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database storing configuration for the OS, hardware, and applications. It is also a common attacker target for persistence.

answer.text: A list of all installed software

feedback.text: Incorrect. Software lists are in Programs and Features or via Get-Package PowerShell cmdlet.

answer.text: A file that contains all user passwords in encrypted form

feedback.text: Incorrect. Password hashes are in the SAM database (a registry hive), but the registry itself is much broader.

answer.text: The Windows security event log

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Security event log is a separate component accessible via Event Viewer.

name.text: OS Security Entry Q3 — Firewall Purpose

questiontext.text: What is the primary purpose of a firewall?

answer.text: To control which network connections are allowed or denied based on rules

feedback.text: Correct! A firewall filters network traffic based on rules (IP, port, protocol, direction), enforcing a network security boundary.

answer.text: To scan files for viruses and malware

feedback.text: Incorrect. That is the function of antivirus/EDR software.

answer.text: To encrypt network traffic between systems

feedback.text: Incorrect. Encryption is handled by protocols like TLS/SSL or VPNs, not firewalls.

answer.text: To monitor user activity and log all actions

feedback.text: Incorrect. User activity monitoring and logging is done by SIEM/UEBA and audit systems.

questiontext.text: In Linux, which file contains user account information including usernames and home directories?

answer.text: /etc/passwd

feedback.text: Correct! /etc/passwd is world-readable and contains: username, UID, GID, GECOS, home directory, and login shell for each account.

answer.text: /etc/shadow

feedback.text: Incorrect. /etc/shadow contains hashed passwords and is readable only by root.

answer.text: /etc/users

feedback.text: Incorrect. There is no standard /etc/users file in Linux.

answer.text: /etc/accounts

feedback.text: Incorrect. There is no standard /etc/accounts file in Linux.

name.text: OS Security Entry Q5 — Least Privilege

answer.text: Only granting users the minimum access necessary to perform their work

feedback.text: Correct! Least privilege limits the blast radius of compromised accounts or insider threats by ensuring no account has more access than strictly needed for its role.

answer.text: Using the simplest password possible so it's easy to remember

feedback.text: Incorrect. Simple passwords are a security risk. Least privilege is about access rights, not passwords.

answer.text: Ensuring all users have equal access to all systems

feedback.text: Incorrect. Equal access to all systems violates least privilege — access should be differentiated by role and need.

answer.text: Requiring administrators to use a separate computer for privileged work

feedback.text: Incorrect. Using privileged access workstations (PAWs) is a good practice, but it is not the definition of least privilege.

questiontext.text: Which Windows process is responsible for managing user authentication and storing credential hashes in memory?

answer.text: lsass.exe

feedback.text: Correct! LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service) handles authentication, stores credential material in memory, and is a primary target for credential dumping attacks.

answer.text: svchost.exe

feedback.text: Incorrect. svchost.exe hosts Windows services but is not responsible for authentication credential storage.

answer.text: winlogon.exe

feedback.text: Incorrect. winlogon.exe handles the login/logout UI but delegates credential validation to lsass.exe.

answer.text: explorer.exe

feedback.text: Incorrect. explorer.exe is the Windows shell (desktop, taskbar) and has no role in authentication.

questiontext.text: In Linux, what does a file permission of chmod 777 grant?

answer.text: Read, write, and execute for the owner, group, and all others

feedback.text: Correct! 777 = rwxrwxrwx — full permissions for everyone. This is rarely appropriate and is a security risk for sensitive files or scripts run by privileged accounts.

answer.text: Read and write for the owner only

feedback.text: Incorrect. Owner-only read/write would be chmod 600.

answer.text: No access for any user

feedback.text: Incorrect. No access would be chmod 000.

answer.text: Full access for root only

feedback.text: Incorrect. chmod 777 grants full access to ALL users, not just root.

questiontext.text: What is the purpose of the Linux /etc/shadow file?

answer.text: To store hashed passwords for user accounts

feedback.text: Correct! /etc/shadow stores password hashes (and aging information). It is readable only by root, unlike /etc/passwd which is world-readable.

answer.text: To store shadow copies of the filesystem

feedback.text: Incorrect. Linux does not use "shadow copies" — that is a Windows concept (Volume Shadow Copy Service).

answer.text: To define sudo permissions

feedback.text: Incorrect. Sudo permissions are defined in /etc/sudoers or /etc/sudoers.d/.

answer.text: To list all running system daemons

feedback.text: Incorrect. Running processes can be viewed with ps, top, or systemctl. /etc/shadow is specifically for password hashes.

answer.text: A tool that allows permitted users to run commands as another user (typically root)

feedback.text: Correct! sudo (superuser do) grants controlled, audited privilege escalation. It logs each use and enforces policy from /etc/sudoers.

answer.text: A command that permanently switches the user to root

feedback.text: Incorrect. sudo runs a single command with elevated privileges; su (without sudo) switches the session. Neither is permanent.

answer.text: A system daemon that manages user accounts

feedback.text: Incorrect. User account management is handled by tools like useradd/usermod and the shadow-utils package.

answer.text: A file that stores root's password

feedback.text: Incorrect. Root's password hash is in /etc/shadow. sudo does not store passwords.

name.text: OS Security Entry Q10 — Event Logs

questiontext.text: What is a Windows Event Log used for?

answer.text: Recording system events, errors, security events, and application activity

feedback.text: Correct! Windows Event Logs (Security, Application, System, etc.) record important activities that are used for troubleshooting, compliance, and security investigations.

answer.text: Storing configuration settings for installed applications

feedback.text: Incorrect. Application configuration is typically stored in the registry or config files, not the Event Log.

answer.text: Managing scheduled maintenance tasks

feedback.text: Incorrect. Scheduled tasks are managed by Task Scheduler, not the Event Log.

answer.text: Storing backup copies of system files

feedback.text: Incorrect. File backups are handled by Windows Backup, VSS, or third-party tools, not the Event Log.

name.text: OS Security Entry Q11 — Discretionary Access Control

questiontext.text: Which of the following best describes "Discretionary Access Control" (DAC)?

answer.text: The owner of a resource decides who has access to it

feedback.text: Correct! In DAC (used by standard Linux/Windows file permissions), the resource owner controls access rights. This contrasts with MAC (Mandatory Access Control), where the OS enforces labels.

answer.text: Access is controlled by the operating system kernel regardless of user settings

feedback.text: Incorrect. That describes Mandatory Access Control (MAC), as implemented by SELinux or AppArmor.

answer.text: Access is granted based on mandatory labels set by a security administrator

feedback.text: Incorrect. Labels-based access control is MAC (Mandatory Access Control), not DAC.

answer.text: All users share the same level of access

feedback.text: Incorrect. Equal access for all is not a named access control model and would be a severe security failure.

name.text: OS Security Entry Q12 — Auditing Purpose

questiontext.text: What is the primary purpose of OS-level auditing?

answer.text: To track and record security-relevant events for detection and forensic investigation

feedback.text: Correct! OS auditing (via auditd on Linux, Security Event Log on Windows) creates an authoritative record of security-relevant actions: logins, privilege use, file access, process creation, etc.

answer.text: To improve system performance by optimizing resource usage

feedback.text: Incorrect. Performance optimization uses profiling tools, not security audit logs.

answer.text: To encrypt user data at rest

feedback.text: Incorrect. Data encryption is handled by tools like BitLocker (Windows) or LUKS (Linux), not audit systems.

answer.text: To manage software updates automatically

feedback.text: Incorrect. Update management is handled by WSUS, apt, yum, etc. — not audit systems.
